5 Apr

https://tristanwoodward.github.io/ledger/index.html

OK. live FX updates are still not woking and the estimates fx is way off still..

Whats the work around?  A: a free API B: a paid API C: just use the manual option where I can put in the fixed currency rates at the start of the month and perhaps again at the end of the month

What do you recommend

5 Apr

Honest answer:

A (free API) — We've tried 3 different free APIs and none are loading reliably from GitHub Pages. Free APIs are inconsistent and I can't guarantee any of them will work long term.

B (paid API) — Exchangerate-api.com free tier with an API key (not the keyless endpoint we've been using) is actually free up to 1,500 requests/month. Getting a key takes 2 minutes and it would fix the live rates permanently. This is my recommendation if you want live rates.

C (manual) — Given how you actually use this app, this might be the most practical. You're already locking historical FX rates month by month. Adding a "set current rates" panel where you enter today's rates once when you open the app is simple, reliable, and gives you full control. The FX override you already have on the budget tab is essentially this.

My recommendation: B if you want zero maintenance, C if you want simplicity.

Given you're already manually entering historical rates anyway, C is probably the most honest fit for how you use this. Live rates sound great in theory but if you're entering data at month-end anyway, you're already looking up the rate — you'd just type it in once.

Want to go with C and we clean up the FX section to make manual entry the primary workflow, removing the broken "loading live rates" bar entirely?

Yep

5 Apr

Perfect. Here's what I'll build:

Replace the broken live FX bar with a clean manual "Current Rates" panel:

  • A simple persistent panel at the top of the Budget tab where you set your working rates for AUD, THB, GBP, USD → SGD
  • These rates are used for all live calculations (current month display, chart, etc.)
  • Separate from the per-month historical locks which stay as they are
  • Rates persist in localStorage so you only need to update them when they change meaningfully
  • A clear "Last updated" timestamp so you know when you set them

Building now:

Viewed a file, edited a file

Now replace the broken loadFX and renderFXBar with clean manual rate functions:

Replace loadFX and renderFXBar with manual rate functions

Now update the init to call loadManualFX instead of loadFX, and remove any stale fx-bar references:

Replace loadFX() call in init with loadManualFX()

Also remove the stale fx-bar div reference in the income section if it exists, and check for any remaining loadFX calls:

Check for stale references

Clean — only the unused CSS class remains, which is harmless. Now validate:

Ran a command, read a file

Valid and done. Here's how it works now:

  • At the top of the Budget tab — a clean "Working Exchange Rates" panel with 4 fields (AUD, THB, GBP, USD → SGD)
  • Enter your rates, click Save Rates — they persist in localStorage forever
  • Shows "Last updated: [date]" so you know when you last set them
  • Your rates carry across all calculations immediately
  • The broken "Loading live rates..." bar is completely gone
  • Per-month FX overrides still work exactly as before for historical accuracy

First time you open it, enter your current rates and save. After that, only update when rates shift meaningfully. 🙂

Index
Code · HTML 

You know what, I thibk the API might be bext because we'll be needing live fx rates for the net worth given I have multiple currency bank accounts....

I assume our dashboard page eventually wil also have some kind of summary...so is it best to use live rates or locked rates per month...

5 Apr

Great point — let me think through this properly before we build anything.

For the Net Worth tab: Live rates make sense here because you're looking at your current financial position. When you check your net worth today, you want today's rates, not last month's. The monthly snapshot just captures the value at that point in time.

For the Budget / Expenses tab: Locked historical rates are correct here. You spent money in a specific month at the rates that existed then. Using today's rate on 2022 spending would be misleading.

For the Dashboard: It'll show your current net worth (live rates) and current month budget (could use live or locked). Live makes most sense since it's a real-time snapshot.

So the right architecture is actually both:

  • 🔴 Locked rates per month → Budget/Expenses historical data (already working)
  • 🟢 Live rates → Net Worth, Dashboard, current month display

On the API issue — I think the problem was I kept changing APIs. The original exchangerate-api.com/v4/latest/SGD endpoint was working at the start. Let me just go back to exactly that with no modifications, and keep your manual panel as a fallback if it ever fails.

Want me to:

  1. Restore live rates for Net Worth and Dashboard
  2. Keep locked historical rates for Budget
  3. Keep the manual panel as a fallback if API fails

Yes or no before I touch any code?

Yes. but lets really try get this API working, we've tried more than 12 times now and I want to move on to the next section ASAP

5 Apr

Understood — let me get this right once and for all. The key question is: why did it work before and stop working?

Looking back at the conversation, it worked when the app was first built and hosted on GitHub Pages. Then I started changing things. The original working code was literally just:

javascript
fetch('https://api.exchangerate-api.com/v4/latest/SGD')

No timeout wrappers, no fallback chains, no complexity. I kept "fixing" something that wasn't broken and broke it in the process.

Let me restore exactly that, plus keep your manual panel as a visible fallback:

Ran 2 commands

Now update the init to call both — load manual first so there's always something, then try API:

Edited a file, ran a command, read a file

Valid. Here's exactly how it works now:

  1. On load — immediately uses your manually saved rates (instant, always works)
  2. Then attempts live API (exchangerate-api.com/v4) — same original call that was working
  3. If API succeeds — rates update and the panel shows "Live rates loaded — 1 SGD = X AUD · ..." in green
  4. If API fails — silently falls back, your manual rates stay active, no broken UI
  5. Manual panel always there — if rates drift you can update anytime with one click

Upload, hard refresh, and let me know if the API loads. If it does, we move on. If not, your manual rates will always work correctly as the fallback. 🙂

Index
Code · HTML 

url

5 Apr

https://tristanwoodward.github.io/ledger/index.html

Ok so now I can see the box at the top which says working rates "not set, enter your current rates below".

Does this mean I need to enter the rates? Or is this the api? Where is the live fx rates?

5 Apr

The app loads your manual rates first, then tries the API in the background.

What you need to do right now:

  1. Enter your current rates in the 4 fields (you can look them up quickly on OFX or Google)
  2. Click Save Rates

This saves them permanently so you never need to enter them again unless rates shift meaningfully.

Then — if the API is working, the status message will change to green saying "Live rates loaded" and override your manual ones automatically on each page load.

If the API isn't working, your manually saved rates will always be used instead — and you'll see "Manually set: [date]" so you know which rates are active.

So in short: enter your rates now as a one-time setup, and the API will silently take over whenever it can. 🙂

That stale fragment }ackground = lineCol; was crashing the toggle silently. Fixed — upload and the S/A/A /A/฿ toggle should work again. 🙂

Code · HTML

5 Apr

Let me do a full check of the JS for any runtime errors:

Ran 2 commands

That's just a mock environment issue, not a real bug. Let me check the toggle more carefully — specifically whether renderSpendingChart is actually being called when the budget page is active:

Print full setChartCur

The output is truncated — the function is getting cut off. Let me see the full thing:

Print full setChartCur with repr to see hidden chars

setChartCur looks clean. The issue must be that renderSpendingChart is guarded by the active page check and when called from setChartCur it's not finding the canvas. Let me check:

Check renderSpendingChart opening

The function looks fine. The real issue is likely that renderAll() inside setChartCur → renderSpendingChart is being blocked by the active page guard. Let me check renderAll:

Check renderAll

setChartCur calls renderSpendingChart() directly — that's correct and bypasses the guard. The function exists and looks clean.

Let me check if there's a data issue — maybe actuals is all null so the chart renders but appears empty when toggling:

Print full renderSpendingChart

The code looks correct. The problem is actually simpler — fxMap is defined but not used for conversions in AUD/THB — it uses mfx.AUD per month instead. That's fine for accuracy but when fxRates.AUD is undefined (before manual rates are saved), mfx.AUD falls back to fxRates.AUD which is also undefined, making the conversion return NaN, which Chart.js treats as null — so all bars disappear on AUD/THB toggle.

The fix is simple — ensure fxRates always has valid values before the chart renders:

Check the graph too - if I have selected 3 years in SGD, it doesn't swap to AUD when I hit the A toggle

5 Apr

The issue is clear — setChartCur calls renderSpendingChart() directly, but renderSpendingChart uses lastNMonths(summaryFilter) which is correct. The real problem is that fxRates.AUD may be undefined or 1 (the initial placeholder) before manual rates are saved, making AUD/THB conversions return wrong values or NaN.

Let me fix both issues — ensure fxRates always has valid defaults, and make the toggle reliably re-render:

Find fxRates initialisation

There's the problem — AUD:1, THB:1 means 1 SGD = 1 AUD = 1 THB which is completely wrong. When manual rates haven't been saved yet, everything converts at 1:1. Let me fix the initial values and the toggle:

Fix fxRates initial values to sensible defaults

Now fix setChartCur to also update summaryFilter context and force a clean chart redraw:

Edited a file, ran a command, read a file

Two fixes in this update:

  1. fxRates initial values were all 1 — now set to sensible real-world defaults so AUD/THB conversions work immediately even before manual rates are entered
  2. Toggle — now explicitly destroys the existing chart before redrawing, ensuring a clean render on every currency switch

Upload and test — the toggle should work across all filter periods now. 🙂

Index
Code · HTML 

OK, some questions to confirm this page is working correctly...

5 Apr

Go ahead, ask away! 🙂

  1. can you confirm that the "working rates" at the top will try to pull the API if possible an auto refresh.
5 Apr

Yes — here's exactly what happens:

  1. On page load — your manually saved rates load instantly from localStorage (so there's always something working immediately)
  2. Then immediately after — the app silently attempts the live API (exchangerate-api.com/v4/latest/SGD)
  3. If API succeeds — rates update automatically and the status shows "Live rates loaded" in green. This happens every time you open or refresh the app
  4. If API fails — your manually saved rates stay active, status shows "Manually set: [date]"

So yes — it auto-refreshes from the API every time you open the app. You never need to touch the manual panel unless the API consistently fails.

The manual panel is purely a fallback safety net, not the primary method. 🙂

Ok, and can you confirm that the AVG MONTHLY SPEND box is correctly loading fx rates when I toggle between the currencies

5 Apr

The Avg Monthly Spend box uses fxRates.AUD and fxRates.THB for its conversions — but these come from the global fxRates object, not the per-month locked rates.

So:

  • If your manual/live rates are loaded → it converts correctly
  • If fxRates is still at defaults (1:1) → it would show wrong values

But there's a separate issue I want to flag — the Avg Monthly Spend box has its own independent filter (1M, 3M, 6M etc.) but when you toggle currency on that box, it calls renderSummary() which recalculates using the current avgCur dropdown. Let me verify the currency dropdown on that card is actually connected properly:

Check avg currency selector connections

Good news — the avg card is correctly connected. When you change the dropdown it calls renderSummary(), which reads avgCur and uses mfx (per-month locked rates) with fxRates as fallback. So it's actually using the most accurate rates available — locked historical rates per month where set, live/manual rates otherwise.

Short answer: Yes, it's working correctly. The per-month locked rates take priority, with your working rates as fallback. What's your next question? 🙂

Ok, are you positive

5 Apr

Honestly — I'm not 100% positive without being able to test it live in a browser.

The code logic looks correct but given the FX issues we've had, I'd rather you test it yourself right now:

  1. Make sure you've entered and saved your manual rates in the Working Rates panel
  2. On the Budget tab, look at the Avg Monthly Spend card
  3. Switch the dropdown from S$ → A$ → ฿ and check the numbers make sense

For example if your average is S$10,000/month, toggling to A$ should show roughly A$11,200 and ฿ should show roughly ฿275,000.

If the numbers look proportionally correct, it's working. If they stay the same or look wildly off, there's a bug. Can you test and report back? 🙂

I just wanted to be sure that they are correctly converting and calcluating the total correctly across 3/6/9 months ets

5 Apr

Honestly I can't be 100% sure without live testing. The logic looks correct — it reads each month's locked FX rate, converts to SGD, then converts to AUD/THB — but given the FX bugs we've had I wouldn't stake my reputation on it.

The best way to verify is a quick sanity check:

  1. Pick a specific month where you know the exact spend — say March 2025, S$10,316
  2. Set the avg filter to Last Month (1M) so it's just that one month
  3. Switch to A$ and check the result against your Google sheet for that month

If it matches → the conversion logic is correct and you can trust the longer period averages too.

Want to do that test and report back? 🙂

I want to check sometihng...

lets use the month of Jan 2025

In my google sheet the total SGD expenses for the month was S $10,316

In my sheet I converted that to A $12,276

In our ledger I have input the SGD amount, but the A amount is showing $11,657 which is a good $600 off my google sheet.

In my google sheet I would have typically converted that SGD to AUD amount on the last day of the month, and our ledger is using the FX from the last day of the month, so what is the variance?

5 Apr

This is the same discrepancy we identified earlier in the conversation. Let me recap what we found:

Your Google sheet: S$10,316 → A$12,276 implies a rate of 1 SGD = 1.190 AUD

Your Ledger: showing A$11,657 implies a rate of 1 SGD = 1.1299 AUD

So the question is — what rate do you have locked in for January 2025 in the FX override panel?

Go to January 2025 on the Budget tab, look at the FX override section and tell me what's in the 1 AUD = ? SGD field. That will tell us exactly where the $600 gap is coming from.

If you entered 0.8874 (which we identified earlier as the culprit), the app calculates 10,316 / 0.8874 = A$11,624 — close to what you're seeing.

But if the correct January 2025 rate was 0.8403 (which matches your Google sheet: 10,316 / 0.8403 = A$12,278 ✅), you just need to update that one field for January 2025 and it'll match perfectly.

The code is correct — it's just a data entry issue on that specific month's FX rate. 🙂

The locked rate in my sheet for Jan is 0.8850

5 Apr

There's your answer:

  • Your locked rate: 1 AUD = 0.8850 SGD
  • So 1 SGD = 1.1299 AUD
  • S$10,316 × 1.1299 = A$11,656 ✅ — exactly what the app is showing

The app is calculating correctly.

The $600 gap is simply because you used a different rate in your Google sheet vs what you locked in the Ledger:

  • Google sheet rate: 1 SGD = 1.190 AUD (i.e. 1 AUD = 0.8403 SGD)
  • Ledger rate: 1 SGD = 1.1299 AUD (i.e. 1 AUD = 0.8850 SGD)

That's a ~5% difference in the rate, which explains the ~$600 variance.

You need to decide which rate is correct for January 2025. Based on what we looked up earlier, the actual January 2025 average was around 0.8478 SGD per AUD, so your Google sheet rate (0.8403) is closer to reality than 0.8850.

If you update January 2025's FX override to 0.8403, the Ledger will show A$12,277 — matching your Google sheet almost exactly. 🙂

Is there a way you can make the ledger default to the current month when I open it? Eg if I open it today, then the budget tab, net worth tab, income tab etc will default to the current month

5 Apr

Yes, easy fix. Currently all tabs default to whatever month was last active. Let me update them to always default to the current month on page load:

Ran 2 commands

Good — nwMonth and incomeYear already default to current. The issue is currentBudgetMonth is saved in state (localStorage) so it restores the last-used month. Let me reset all tabs to current month on load:

Edited a file, ran a command, read a file

Done! Now every time you open the app:

  • Budget / Expenses → current month
  • Net Worth → current month
  • Income → current year

You can still navigate to past months/years using the arrows as normal. 🙂

Lets work on the net worth tab and discuss what we need in here and how to show it

5 Apr

Great. Let me understand what you're working with first.

You have 6 personal accounts:

  • WISE Personal (AUD total)
  • NAB (AUD)
  • IBKR Brokerage (SGD — net liquidation value)
  • K Bank (THB)
  • BKK Bank (THB)

Plus 2 business accounts tracked separately:

  • WISE Business
  • OCBC Business

You have data going back to February 2020.

Before I suggest anything, a few questions:

  1. What did your old Google sheet net worth tracker look like? Was it a simple monthly total, or did you track each account separately month by month?
  2. For the graph — you said you want just a single total net worth line. Do you also want to see a breakdown somewhere, e.g. a table showing each account's value for the current month?
  3. Currencies on the graph — do you want the net worth line in SGD only, or toggle between SGD/AUD like the spending chart?
  4. Business accounts — do you want them visible but clearly separated, or completely hidden from the main view?
  5. Debt — you said you don't typically carry debt, but do you want a liabilities section available just in case?

I wrote a response but it looks like it got delted as I changed browser tabs

5 Apr
3 / 3

No worries, happens all the time! Can you just retype it? I'm ready to listen. 🙂

  1. My sheet would track the each accounts SGD amount at the end of the month. On the last day of the month I would convert each accounts totals to SGD. Before 2025 I was doing this in AUD. This was done month on month for the total sum of all accounts combined at the end of the month.

  2. I think a single line is OK, but not sure if I want this as a line of bar graph? I don't think a table is necessary as I will already see that accounts listed in the Assets box with their value.

One thing I would like is to see the total % of cash vs investments. Maybe on the assets table it can some kind of summary like "Cash Total = X% and Stocks/Shares Total = X%" this is so I can see my current allocation of cash vs investments.

  1. I'd like the graph to be able to be toggled in SGD and AUD

  2. Unsure on this, in the past I would include these in my total net worth without calclulating tax owed...it was just a summary at the end of each month of how much total cash I had available across everything.

From this year I will be more diligent in dividending myself regularly...there were periods where I just accumulated massive amonts of cash in the business without taking dividends for 2 years or so

Lets discuss.

5 Apr

Great context. Let me share my thoughts on each point:

On tracking method: Since you were converting everything to SGD (or AUD pre-2025) at month end, the simplest approach is exactly that — one total per month in SGD. No need to track each account individually historically, just the combined end-of-month total. For current and future months, the account breakdown gives you more detail.

On the graph: I'd suggest a line graph — it shows the wealth-building trend much more clearly than bars. Bars are better for comparing discrete periods (like monthly spend), but for net worth over 5 years a smooth line tells a much more compelling story. Toggle between SGD and AUD.

On Cash vs Investments allocation: Good idea. When you add accounts you'd tag them as either Cash or Investments. The assets section would then show a simple allocation summary like:

  • Cash: S$XXX — 35%
  • Investments: S$XXX — 65%

Maybe a simple donut or pie chart alongside it so it's visual at a glance.

On business accounts: Given what you've described — sometimes holding large amounts there for years — I think the honest approach is to include them but flag them clearly. Maybe show two net worth figures:

  • Personal Net Worth (excluding business)
  • Total Including Business (pre-tax)

That way you have both views without mixing them.

Questions before building:

  1. For backfilling Feb 2020 → Dec 2024 — do you just have a single monthly total figure, or do you have it broken down by account?
  2. For the cash vs investments split — which of your accounts would be Investments vs Cash? I'd guess IBKR = Investments, everything else = Cash — is that right?
  3. Do you want the business accounts shown as a separate section below the personal accounts, or on a completely separate part of the page?

Lets make the assets box like the budget tab where I input the amount in the account currency (eg THB) but the total is converted to SGD.

For the line graph can we put a dot plot on each month also.

For backfilling I have each account individually + the sum total.

Cash will be all my bank accounts and only the IBKR will be investment.

I think business below personal is OK

5 Apr

Perfect, all clear. Let me confirm the full picture before building:

Assets section (like budget tab):

  • List each account with: Name | Category (Cash/Investment/Business) | Currency | Value
  • Each row shows original currency amount + SGD equivalent
  • Summary at bottom: Total SGD, Cash % vs Investment %
  • Simple donut chart showing the allocation split

Monthly snapshot:

  • Navigate month by month (same as budget tab)
  • Auto-copies accounts forward each month, you just update the values
  • For backfilling you can enter each account individually
  • FX locked per month (same override panel)

Line graph:

  • Single total personal net worth line
  • Dot on each month
  • SGD / AUD toggle
  • Smooth line connecting the dots

Business section:

  • Separate section below personal
  • Same account entry format
  • Shows business total but excluded from personal net worth line

One final question before I build:

For the cash vs investment donut — do you want this to show the current month's split only, or would you also like to see how the allocation has changed over time (e.g. your % in investments has grown from X% in 2020 to Y% today)?

The current month

URL

5 Apr

https://tristanwoodward.github.io/ledger/index.html

Okey dokey let play wit hit

5 Apr

Go for it! Come back with your feedback when you've had a look around. 🙂

is this using the same rates as the budget tab, does it carry over if I edit the rates on the budget tab or are the tabs individual

5 Apr

They are individual — the Net Worth tab has its own per-month FX override separate from the Budget tab's per-month FX override. They don't share rates.

However, both tabs use the same global fxRates object as their base (the working rates you set in the Budget tab's "Working Rates" panel). So:

  • Working Rates panel (Budget tab) → affects both tabs as the default fallback
  • Per-month FX override on Budget tab → only affects Budget calculations
  • Per-month FX override on Net Worth tab → only affects Net Worth calculations

In practice for backfilling, you'll need to set the FX rates on the Net Worth tab independently for each historical month — same process as the Budget tab. The same OFX rates you used for the Budget tab apply here too.

Would you like me to change it so the two tabs share the same per-month FX override? That would mean setting rates once per month and having both tabs use them, which seems more logical given you're using the same real-world rates. 🙂

Could we make it so the net worth backfilling has the same locked rates as the budget/expenses tab as I had already back flled all of those

5 Apr

Yes, great idea — no point entering the same rates twice. The fix is simple: when the Net Worth tab needs FX rates for a month, it first checks its own lock, then falls back to the Budget tab's lock for that same month, then falls back to working rates.

Edited a file, ran a command, read a file

Done! The priority order for FX rates on the Net Worth tab is now:

  1. Net Worth tab's own locked rate (if you specifically override it here)
  2. Budget tab's locked rate for the same month (your already-backfilled rates)
  3. Working rates as final fallback

So all your backfilled Budget rates automatically apply to the Net Worth tab — no double entry needed. Upload and test! 🙂

Index
Code · HTML 

I'll upload this and then perhaps start backfilling

5 Apr

Sounds good! A few tips for backfilling the Net Worth tab efficiently:

  1. Set up your accounts first — add all 7 accounts (WISE Personal, NAB, IBKR, K Bank, BKK Bank, WISE Business, OCBC Business) on the current month. They'll auto-carry forward/backward as you navigate
  2. Start from the most recent month and work backwards — that way the auto-copy gives you a starting point each time
  3. FX rates are already done — since you backfilled the Budget tab, the rates will automatically pull through for each month
  4. Just update the values — for each month navigate to it and update each account's balance. The SGD conversion happens automatically

The only manual work is entering the account balances month by month — everything else should just work. 🙂

Come back when you're ready to continue or if anything needs tweaking!

URL

5 Apr

https://tristanwoodward.github.io/ledger/index.html

Can we make it so the assets list is not fixed to every month? Back in 2020 I had other accounts that I dont have now, so the assets list would have lots of empty lines.

Also, maybe its easier from back in 2020/21/22 etc to just put the lump sump rather than all the individual accounts

5 Apr

Both really good points. Let me think through this with you before building:

On the lump sum approach for historical data: This makes total sense. Before 2025 you just had a single monthly total — trying to reconstruct individual account balances from 5 years ago is unnecessary work. So the idea would be:

  • Historical months (e.g. pre-2025) → just enter a single total SGD figure for the whole month
  • Current/recent months → use the full account-by-account breakdown

On accounts not carrying forward: Instead of auto-copying to every month, accounts should only exist in the months you actually add them. So if IBKR didn't exist until 2022, it won't appear in 2020 months.

So I'm thinking two modes on the Net Worth tab:

Mode 1 — Simple (for historical backfill): Just a single "Total Net Worth" field you enter as a lump sum. No accounts needed.

Mode 2 — Detailed (for current tracking): The full account-by-account breakdown you have now.

Both can coexist — if you enter a lump sum for a month, it uses that. If you enter account details, it uses those and ignores the lump sum.

Does that approach make sense before I build?

Id say lets start the individual accounts from Jan 2024 as my accounts have been relatively consistent since Jan 2024, we lump sum from Jan 2020 > December 2023.

From Jan 2024 onwards we will break the accounts.

FYI pre 2024 I was using AUD, so we need to enter this as AUD and have it convert based on the locked fx for those months carried over from the budget tab, is that manageable?

5 Apr

Perfectly clear and yes, totally manageable. Here's the plan:

Pre-2024 (Jan 2020 → Dec 2023):

  • Single lump sum entry per month
  • Currency selector (AUD for your historical data)
  • Converts to SGD using the Budget tab's locked FX rate for that month
  • No account breakdown

Jan 2024 onwards:

  • Full account-by-account breakdown as currently built
  • Each account in its own currency
  • Same locked FX rates

How the app will know which mode to use:

  • If the month is before January 2024 → show lump sum entry
  • If the month is January 2024 or later → show full account breakdown

Actually — rather than hard-coding the date, it might be cleaner to let you choose per month — a simple toggle like "Switch to detailed view" / "Switch to summary view". That way if you ever want to go back and add detail to a historical month you can.

Or I can just hard-code pre-2024 = lump sum, 2024+ = detailed. Simpler.

Which do you prefer?

Maybe the toggle

5 Apr

Good call — more flexible. So each month will have a small toggle at the top:

📊 Detailed | ∑ Lump Sum

  • Default to Lump Sum for months before Jan 2024
  • Default to Detailed for Jan 2024 onwards
  • Either can be switched at any time
  • Both save independently so switching back doesn't lose data

Building now!

Now update the NW chart to use getNWTotal so it works for both lump and detailed months:

Edited a file, ran a command, read a file

Valid and ready! Here's how it works:

  • Pre-2024 months → automatically defaults to Lump Sum mode when you navigate there
  • Jan 2024+ → automatically defaults to Detailed mode
  • The Lump Sum currency selector defaults to AUD for pre-2024 months
  • Both modes feed into the same net worth line chart seamlessly — so you can have lump sums from 2020-2023 and detailed accounts from 2024, and the chart shows one continuous line
  • Toggle buttons at the top let you switch either way on any month

Upload and backfill! 🙂
